segments.title: Why Malta was not simply a British Midway
segments.summary: The Malta class is often compared with the American Midways, but this opening separates a superficial resemblance from the Royal Navy's own design history.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke challenges the claim that Malta was merely a copied Midway design. Both were large carriers conceived once treaty restrictions no longer set the ceiling, but Malta emerged from a separate British sequence of studies. The comparison is useful only when it keeps those parallel national design problems in view.

evidence.note: The lecture disputes the claim that Malta was a carbon copy of Midway.

segments.title: Treaty limits and the Royal Navy's ideal carrier
segments.summary: Pre-war carrier studies began with an ideal design, then worked back to what treaty tonnage would permit.
segments.body: British carrier planning did not begin at the treaty ceiling. Designers explored the carrier they would prefer to build, calculated its tonnage, and then developed treaty-compliant alternatives. The surviving studies show how figures such as Stanley Goodall and Reginald Henderson connected operational ambitions to the compromises imposed by treaty limits.

evidence.note: National Archives design studies compare treaty-limited carriers with unrestricted options.
Malta and Midway are framed as designs freed from treaty constraints.

segments.title: A carrier scaled for larger wartime aircraft
segments.summary: Malta retained the ambition for four lifts, a very large hangar, a large air group, and protection, but adjusted the concept for aircraft that had grown during the war.
segments.body: The 1942 Malta work was not a frozen pre-war ideal: aircraft size and naval aviation requirements had changed sharply. A carrier's hangar is fixed once built, while successive aircraft generations consume more space and room to manoeuvre. Malta's scale was therefore intended to preserve useful air-group capacity while supporting larger, more capable aircraft.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the design requirements and why larger aircraft reduce a fixed hangar's capacity.

segments.title: The Future Building Committee and the post-war fleet
segments.summary: Admiral Purvis's Future Building Committee revived long-range fleet thinking by pairing experienced officers with earlier Henderson-era concepts.
segments.body: After Henderson's death, wartime construction management made it harder to sustain long-range naval planning. Admiral Sir Charles Edward Kennedy Purvis chaired a committee intended to look beyond immediate building work, drawing on the best available people and earlier design concepts. Dr. Clarke presents the Malta programme as part of that broader effort to shape the Royal Navy's future fleet.

evidence.note: Purvis's appointment, the Future Building Committee, and its use of earlier concepts are described.

segments.title: Lifts, stability, and the armoured hangar deck
segments.summary: This design discussion links centreline lifts, hull stability, a huge flight deck, and Malta's decision to place armour on the hangar deck.
segments.body: Deck-edge lifts could improve aircraft handling, but their off-centre weight imposed a stability penalty, helping explain the Royal Navy's preference for centreline arrangements in earlier carriers. Malta's exceptionally large flight deck also required an expansion joint and a twin-island layout. Since an armoured deck could not conveniently incorporate that joint, the design used a strong but unarmoured flight deck and made the hangar deck the armoured strength deck.

evidence.note: The transcript explains deck-edge lift weight, ballast, and centreline lift advantages.
The lecture explains the expansion joint, twin islands, and armoured hangar deck.

segments.title: The aviation facilities Malta was meant to support
segments.summary: A high hangar, four shafts, high speed, and an air group of roughly 80 to 108 aircraft show the operational scale envisioned for Malta.
segments.body: The tall hangar had two related purposes: accommodating larger American and British aircraft and providing working height for maintenance crews and cranes. Four shafts reflected a Royal Navy preference for redundancy and survivability in major warships. The upper air-group figure depended on deck parking, which Dr. Clarke treats as much more practical in the Pacific than in the Mediterranean or North Atlantic.

evidence.note: The transcript covers hangar height, maintenance, four shafts, speed, and the projected air group.
A proposed lift arrangement is compared with the original arrangement.

segments.title: Why the Royal Navy did not begin with Malta-sized carriers
segments.summary: Treaties and production efficiency favoured repeated, adaptable designs; Malta represented a deliberate break toward a large post-war fleet carrier.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that changing designs repeatedly can slow wartime production and introduce inefficiency, even when each revision looks better on paper. The light fleet carriers demonstrate the opposite approach: adapt a known design and build it in quantity. Malta was different because it was intended as a new, large carrier at the centre of global Royal Navy deployment, with value that extended beyond the immediate war.

evidence.note: The lecture contrasts design continuity in light fleet carriers with Malta's intended strategic role.

segments.title: Cancellation and Malta's post-war counterfactual
segments.summary: After Malta was cancelled in late 1945, the smaller Audacious carriers continued; the closing argument asks how several larger Maltas might have changed post-war carrier policy.
segments.body: The Malta ships were cancelled in October and December 1945 while the Audacious programme was carried forward in reduced form. Dr. Clarke stresses that Malta's much greater displacement and hangar volume offered more room to absorb future aircraft growth. He then frames the central counterfactual: had three Maltas entered service, their value to Britain and NATO might have altered later decisions about the Royal Navy's carrier force.

evidence.note: The transcript covers cancellation, Audacious, and Malta's greater displacement and future growth margin.
The lecture considers wartime completion and the post-war three-Malta counterfactual.
